turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Thomas has the opinion of the Court this morning in Case 22-913, DeVillier versus Texas.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: This case comes to us on a writ of certiorari to the United States Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit. Richard DeVillier, and more than 120 other petitioners, own property north of Interstate 10 between Houston and Beaumont, Texas. The State of Texas decided to use portions of the highway as a flood evacuation route. To that end, it installed a 3-foot barrier along the highway median to act as a dam during those storms. The dam worked, but it flooded petitioners' property. Petitioners brought claims based on the Takings Clause. The Fifth Circuit held that the Takings Clause does not provide a right of action for takings claims against the state. Petitioners ask us to determine whether a person whose property is taken without compensation may seek redress under the Takings Clause even if the legislature has not affirmatively provided a cause of action. In an opinion filed with the Clerk today, we vacate and remand. Constitutional rights do not generally come with a built-in cause of action that allows for private enforcement in Courts. They are typically asserted offensively using an independent cause of action designed for that purpose. This case asked what would happen if a property owner had no cause of action to vindicate his rights under the Takings Clause, but it would be imprudent to decide that question without first satisfying ourselves of the premise that these petitioners have no such cause of action. Texas confirmed at oral argument that its state law in inverse-condemnation cause of action allows petitioner to bring their claims under the Takings Clause. This case therefore does not present the circumstance in which a property owner has no cause of action to seek just compensation. On remand, the petitioner should be permitted to pursue all of their claims under the Takings Clause through the cause of action available under Texas law. For these reasons and others set forth in our opinion, we vacate and remand to the Fifth Circuit for further proceedings consistent with this opinion. The opinion of the Court is unanimous.
